A winter storm dropped heavy snow and sleet as well as some freezing rain across most of New Jersey, except for the immediate coast. Snowfall and sleet totals average 10 to 20 inches from around the Interstate 95 corridor northwestward with the highest amounts in Warren and Sussex Counties. Elsewhere, snowfall and sleet amounts dropped considerably the closer to the coast municipalities were located. Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch. Little if any snow or sleet accumulations occurred along the coastal strips of Cape May and Atlantic Counties. ||Snow overspread New Jersey from south to north between 9 p.m. EST and Midnight EST on the 12th. Three bands of heavy snow, in which snowfall rates averaged two to three inches per hour, moved through the state. The first one mainly affected the southern third of the state around 3 a.m. EST on the 13th. The second one moved through the entire southern half of the state (away from the coast where it was rain by then) between 4 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST on the 13th. The last one formed in the southern half of the state around 6 a.m. EST on the 13th and proceeded to move north through the entire state by 2 p.m. EST on the 13th. After the snow bands moved through, precipitation changed to rain in the southeast part of the state during the morning of the 13th, the southwest and central part of the state during the second half of the morning and around midday on the 13th and during the afternoon of the 13th in the northwest part of the state. Some pockets of freezing persisted. Thunderstorms moved through the state during the late afternoon and early evening on the 13th, some with small hail. Then as the low pressure system pulled offshore, precipitation changed back to snow during the second half of the evening on the 13th and ended after Midnight EST on the 14th. The last snow band exited New Jersey between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 14th. ||Governor Chris Christie declared a state of emergency and New Jersey State Offices were closed and non-essential personnel did not have to report to work. Many schools, malls, businesses, other government and  local offices as well as municipal courts never opened on the 13th. Many schools in the northern part of the state were also closed on the 14th. New Jersey State offices opened late on the 14th. There were speed restrictions on the New Jersey Turnpike and the Delaware River crossings.  The early start to the heavy snow cut down on the number of accidents. The New Jersey State Police responded to 334 motor vehicle accidents and 632 calls for assistance. Many of the flights into and out of the Atlantic City International Airport were cancelled. The snow did not cling to tree limbs as well and there were only about 400 homes and businesses that lost power in Public Service Electric and Gas's service area.  One of the hardest hit businesses were florists as the heavy snow wreaked havoc (tens of thousands delayed) on the flower deliveries for Valentine's Day.  The latest event strained municipal budgets further and increased the shortage of salt supplies in the region.  The weight of the snow started to cause structural damage. In Raritan (Somerset County), strip mall roofing collapsed on U.S. Route 202. A winter storm dropped heavy snow and sleet as well as some freezing rain across most of New Jersey, except for the immediate coast. Snowfall and sleet totals average 10 to 20 inches from around the Interstate 95 corridor northwestward with the highest amounts in Warren and Sussex Counties. Elsewhere, snowfall and sleet amounts dropped considerably the closer to the coast municipalities were located. Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch. Little if any snow or sleet accumulations occurred along the coastal strips of Cape May and Atlantic Counties. ||Snow overspread New Jersey from south to north between 9 p.m. EST and Midnight EST on the 12th. Three bands of heavy snow, in which snowfall rates averaged two to three inches per hour, moved through the state. The first one mainly affected the southern third of the state around 3 a.m. EST on the 13th. The second one moved through the entire southern half of the state (away from the coast where it was rain by then) between 4 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST on the 13th. The last one formed in the southern half of the state around 6 a.m. EST on the 13th and proceeded to move north through the entire state by 2 p.m. EST on the 13th. After the snow bands moved through, precipitation changed to rain in the southeast part of the state during the morning of the 13th, the southwest and central part of the state during the second half of the morning and around midday on the 13th and during the afternoon of the 13th in the northwest part of the state. Some pockets of freezing persisted. Thunderstorms moved through the state during the late afternoon and early evening on the 13th, some with small hail. Then as the low pressure system pulled offshore, precipitation changed back to snow during the second half of the evening on the 13th and ended after Midnight EST on the 14th. The last snow band exited New Jersey between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 14th. ||Governor Chris Christie declared a state of emergency and New Jersey State Offices were closed and non-essential personnel did not have to report to work. Many schools, malls, businesses, other government and  local offices as well as municipal courts never opened on the 13th. Many schools in the northern part of the state were also closed on the 14th. New Jersey State offices opened late on the 14th. There were speed restrictions on the New Jersey Turnpike and the Delaware River crossings.  The early start to the heavy snow cut down on the number of accidents. The New Jersey State Police responded to 334 motor vehicle accidents and 632 calls for assistance. Many of the flights into and out of the Atlantic City International Airport were cancelled. The snow did not cling to tree limbs as well and there were only about 400 homes and businesses that lost power in Public Service Electric and Gas's service area.  One of the hardest hit businesses were florists as the heavy snow wreaked havoc (tens of thousands delayed) on the flower deliveries for Valentine's Day.  The latest event strained municipal budgets further and increased the shortage of salt supplies in the region.  The weight of the snow started to cause structural damage. In Raritan (Somerset County), strip mall roofing collapsed on U.S. Route 202. No injuries occurred. ||Representative snowfall included 22.8 inches in Montague Township (Sussex County), 19.2 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 19.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 17.3 inches in Rockaway Township (Morris County), 17.2 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 16.0 inches in Butler (Morris County), 15.4 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 15.3 inches in Boonton (Morris County), 15.2 inches in Bridgewater (Somerset County), 14.5 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 14.4 inches in Greenwich Township (Warren County), 14.0 inches in Kingwood Township (Hunterdon County), 13.8 inches in Bound Brook (Somerset County), 13.1 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County), 12.7 inches in Florence (Burlington County), 12.2 inches in Westampton Township (Burlington County), 11.6 inches in Washington Township (Gloucester County) and Readington Township (Hunterdon County), 11.5 inches in Clinton Township (Hunterdon County), 11.2 inches in South Plainfield (Middlesex County), 10.6 inches in Burlington Township (Burlington County), 10.5 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 10.0 inches in Moorestown (Burlington County) and Ewing (Mercer County), 9.4 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County), 8.9 inches in Jackson Township (Ocean County), 8.6 inches in Woodstown (Salem County), 8.5 inches in Pitman (Gloucester County),  8.3 inches in Tabernacle (Burlington County), 7.6 inches in Egg Harbor City (Atlantic County), 7.5 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 4.0 inches in Woodbine (Cape May County), 3.3 inches in Berkeley Township (Ocean County), 3.0 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport and 2.2 inches in Lavallette (Ocean County).| |The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the 11th and moved east on the 12th. This was preceded by an arctic high pressure system that passed over the Middle Atlantic States that same day and left plenty of initial cold, dry air in place. A new low pressure system formed east of Florida on the 12th and would become the primary low pressure system for the event. It moved northeast and was near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 12th. From there, it continued northnortheast and passed near Elizabeth City, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 13th. Once it reached the Atlantic again, it started to intensify rapidly and reached the Delmarva coastal waters early in the afternoon on the 13th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 13th, the low pressure system was off the New Jersey coast and deepened to 986 millibars (11 millibar drop in 12 hours). From there the low pressure system moved northeast and intensified even more rapidly as it passed just east of Long Island, New York and passed near Boston, Massachusetts as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 4 a.m. EST on the 14th. This low pressure system deepened to 968 millibars at 10 a.m. EST on the 14th while over Maine before finally leveling off. The close pass to the New Jersey coast permitted this low pressure system to bring in above freezing (milder) air into the state, if not at the surface, at least aloft and caused a period of non-snow to occur before ending as snow again as it departed.

Snowfall and sleet totals average 10 to 20 inches from around the Interstate 95 corridor northwestward with the highest amounts in Warren and Sussex Counties. The first one mainly affected the southern third of the state around 3 a.m. EST on the 13th. The second one moved through the entire southern half of the state (away from the coast where it was rain by then) between 4 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST on the 13th. The last one formed in the southern half of the state around 6 a.m. EST on the 13th and proceeded to move north through the entire state by 2 p.m. EST on the 13th. After the snow bands moved through, precipitation changed to rain in the southeast part of the state during the morning of the 13th, the southwest and central part of the state during the second half of the morning and around midday on the 13th and during the afternoon of the 13th in the northwest part of the state. Some pockets of freezing persisted. Thunderstorms moved through the state during the late afternoon and early evening on the 13th, some with small hail. A new low pressure system formed east of Florida on the 12th and would become the primary low pressure system for the event. It moved northeast and was near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 12th. From there, it continued northnortheast and passed near Elizabeth City, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 13th. Once it reached the Atlantic again, it started to intensify rapidly and reached the Delmarva coastal waters early in the afternoon on the 13th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 13th, the low pressure system was off the New Jersey coast and deepened to 986 millibars (11 millibar drop in 12 hours). From there the low pressure system moved northeast and intensified even more rapidly as it passed just east of Long Island, New York and passed near Boston, Massachusetts as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 4 a.m. EST on the 14th. This low pressure system deepened to 968 millibars at 10 a.m. EST on the 14th while over Maine before finally leveling off. The close pass to the New Jersey coast permitted this low pressure system to bring in above freezing (milder) air into the state, if not at the surface, at least aloft and caused a period of non-snow to occur before ending as snow again as it departed.

An accumulating snow that fell throughout most of New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 18th caused slippery and hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways.  The precipitation fell as sleet and freezing rain in parts of interior southeast New Jersey toward the end of the event. Snowfall averaged 1 to 4 inches with the highest snowfall over the Pine Barrens and the lowest snowfall in Sussex (too far northwest of the heavier precipitation) and Cape May (more rain than snow) Counties. Ice accumulations were less than one-tenth of an inch. Snow overspread the state from south to north between 200 a.m. EST and 500 a.m. EST on the 18th. Southeast of the Pine Barrens, the snow transitioned from snow to sleet and then rain (some freezing rain inland) around 6 a.m. EST. In the southern half of the state, precipitation ended between 7 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST. The very persistent and strong west to northwest winds that started during the afternoon of the 27th coupled with the greater spring tides cycles associated with the approaching new moon caused blowout tides during the overnight low tide cycle on the 27th along north coastal New Jersey. At Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), the low tide reached 1.97 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tide conditions start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The winds decreased on the 28th and while tides were below astronomical levels, additional blowout conditions did not occur.

A developing cold front triggered snow showers in New Jersey during the morning into the early afternoon of the 26th. The snow showers and snowfall were greatest in the Passaic and Raritan Basins and the Philadelphia eastern and northeastern suburbs averaging 1 to 2 inches. Elsewhere in New Jersey, snowfall averaged less than 1 inch. The snow showers started between 6 a.m. and 9 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest in the second half of the morning and finished moving through between Noon and 1 p.m. EST that afternoon. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Randolph Township (Morris County), 1.8 inches in Frenchtown (Hunterdon County), 1.6 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Medford (Burlington County), Montgomery (Somerset County), South Plainfield (Middlesex County), Mendham (Morris County)  and Somerdale (Camden County), 1.3 inches in North Brunswick (Middlesex County), 1.1 inches in National Park (Gloucester County), 1.0 inch in Hopewell Township (Mercer County) and 0.3 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through the state from the northwest to southeast between 9 a.m. EST and 1 p.m. EST on the 26th and nearly coincided with the end of the snow showers.

A lee side trof combined with an unseasonably warm air mass to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 27th. While the thunderstorms started to weaken as they approached New Jersey, their gust fronts still produced some damaging winds in the central third of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Moorestown Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof combined with an unseasonably warm air mass to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 27th. While the thunderstorms started to weaken as they approached New Jersey, their gust fronts still produced some damaging winds in the central third of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees onto wires in Clarksburg (Millstone Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracking along a stalled frontal boundary extending across the central Appalachians eastward through the Mid Atlantic States produced multiple waves of heavy rain over sections of central to southern New Jersey from the late evening on the 29th through the late evening on the 30th, with some showers lasting into the morning of the 31st before low pressure tracked east and away from the coast.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation caused some poor drainage and roadway flooding.  Some streams and small rivers also overflowed their banks on the 30th and the 31st, with some not returning below flood stage until the first couple of days into April.  Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 4 inches across central to southern New Jersey.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused minor tidal flooding along the northern New Jersey coast during the evening high tide cycle on the 30th.  At Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), the highest tide reached 6.88 feet above mean lower low water at 812 p.m. EDT on the 30th.  Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from waves of heavy precipitation caused minor flooding along the North Branch Rancocas from the late evening of the 30th through the late evening of April 1st. The North Branch Rancocas at Pemberton was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 1045 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 1030 p.m. EDT on April 1st, and it crested at 2.65 feet at 530 p.m. EDT on the 31st. Event precipitation totals included 3.08 inches in Moorestown Township, 3.06 inches in Riverton, 2.96 inches in Mount Laurel Township, 2.85 inches in Tabernacle Township, 2.81 inches in Medford Lakes, 2.73 inches in Burlington, and 2.70 inches at WFO Mount Holly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Creek and river flooding from the heavy rain that occurred from the evening of March 29th into the evening of March 30th persisted through the evening and night of April 1st in parts of Burlington, Gloucester, Salem and Somerset Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from waves of heavy precipitation caused minor flooding along the Maurice River from the evening of March 31st to the early morning of April 2nd. The Maurice River at Norma was above its 4 foot flood stage from 745 p.m. EDT on March 31st through 1230 a.m. EDT on April 2nd, and it crested at 4.06 feet at 945 a.m. EDT on April 1st. Event precipitation totals included 3.04 inches in Pittsgrove Township, 2.81 inches in Woodstown and 2.46 inches in Pennsville Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire occurred in Burlington County on the afternoon of the 19th in Lumberton Township. The fire started about 1230 p.m. EDT that afternoon and consumed about 25 wooded acres just west of New Jersey State Route 38 in the township. State Forest Fire personnel as well as firefighters from five townships battled the blaze. It was declared under control at 330 p.m. EDT that afternoon. Portions of westbound New Jersey State Route 38 as well as U.S. Route 206 were closed because of poor visibility. The flames threatened vehicles at an automobile salvage and repair yard. They were moved to safety and no injuries or damage other than fencing damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on April 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in central New Jersey. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Flooding in parts of the Passaic Basin and southeast New Jersey did not start until May began. Major creek and river flooding occurred in the Raritan and Assunpink Basins with moderate river flooding occurring in parts of the Passaic Basin. Many roadways were flooded. New Jersey Transit cross honored tickets on the commuter rail lines and buses. One hundred forty-six major roadways and interstates were flooded and completely closed or had lane closures throughout the state of New Jersey. Many roadways were re-opened by the end of the day on May 1st and most were opened again on May 2nd. The steady rain started during the afternoon of April 29th and fell at a steady light to moderate clip through the night. But, on April 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of April 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. ||The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey. But the formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and river and stream flooding in Monmouth County. The flooding on the creeks and rivers in some locations were the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. New Jersey State Route 35 was hardest hit by the flooding as it was entirely closed in parts of Middletown, Eatontown and Hazlet Townships. New Jersey State Route 71 had lane closures in Deal. The Manasquan River at Squankum had moderate flooding and was above its 7.5 foot flood stage from 1036 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 733 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 9.84 feet at 830 a.m. EDT on the 1st, the 6th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. Farther downstream, the Manasquan River at Allenwood was above its 14.5 foot flood stage from 158 a.m. EDT through 8 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 16.57 feet at 1015 a.m. EDT on May 1st, the 3rd highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. The Swimming River at Red Bank had minor flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1109 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 1010 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 7.73 feet at 315 a.m. EDT on the 1st . The Shark River at Neptune City had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1212 a.m. EDT through 500 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.18 feet at 130 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 5.20 inches in Millstone Township, 4.93 inches in Eatontown, 4.67 inches in Red Bank, 4.58 inches in Keyport and 4.33 inches in Howell Township and Cream Ridge.

The North Branch Rancocas at Pemberton was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 1045 p.m. EDT on March 30th through 1030 p.m. EDT on April 1st, and it crested at 2.65 feet at 530 p.m. EDT on March 31st. Event precipitation totals included 3.08 inches in Moorestown Township, 3.06 inches in Riverton, 2.96 inches in Mount Laurel Township, 2.85 inches in Tabernacle Township, 2.81 inches in Medford Lakes, 2.73 inches in Burlington, and 2.70 inches at WFO Mount Holly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms accompanying a cold front moved through New Jersey during the afternoon of the 15th.  Isolated wind damage occurred in Somerset County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large trees on one property on Valley Road in Watchung. One of the downed trees damaged a home. In addition, a gazebo on the property was flipped and tossed about 20 feet. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient (difference) between an intensifying strong low pressure system and a high pressure system in the Ohio Valley caused high to strong northwest winds to occur across New Jersey from the evening of the 12th following a cold frontal passage through the daytime hours on the 13th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph, with some gusts as high as around 60 mph.  The strongest winds occurred overnight on the 12th. The high winds toppled a tree that killed one man in Sussex County and the high winds damaged the roof of one store in Morris County. In addition, the prolonged period of strong winds caused weak tree limbs, trees and power lines to be knocked down and caused widely scattered power outages.  About 1,700 homes and businesses lost power and a number of roadways were closed because of downed trees and limbs.||In Sussex County, a 74-year-old man was killed when an 80 foot tall tree fell completely through his home on Maxim Drive in Hopatcong on the afternoon of the 13th. The tree also damaged the neighboring home. Roofing debris on a kerosene heater started a small fire. A police officer rescued two dogs in the house and was injured extinguishing the fire. In Morris County, a piece of roofing material of a Bed, Bath and Beyond store on International Drive in Flanders was ripped away. The roofing debris struck a gas line and started a small fire. The mall was evacuated as a precaution.  In Burlington County, a downed tree blocked U.S. Route 206 in Mansfield Township. In Camden County, a downed tree damaged a business sign in Camden. Another downed tree blocked Kearsarge Road in Camden.||Peak wind gusts included 62 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 59 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and Wantage (Sussex County), 58 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 56 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 52 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and Brick Township (Ocean County), 50 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 49 mph at the McGuire Air Force Base (Burlington County), 48 mph in Newport (Cumberland County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Absecon (Atlantic County), 46 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and Belmar (Monmouth County) and 45 mph in Lumberton (Burlington County). ||The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds emerged from the Rockies on the evening of the 10th. It reached central Missouri on the evening of the 11th and western Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 12th. It already was an intense 984 millibar low pressure system. The northeast moving 982 millibar low pressure system reached central New Jersey at 8 p.m. EDT on the 12th as its cold front as moving through the state. The 981 millibar low pressure system was near Boston, Massachusetts at 11 p.m. EDT on the 12th and reached Nova Scotia as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 5 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It continued to move northeast through the Canadian Maritimes during the day on the 13th and very slowly weakened. By evening on the 13th, the high pressure system was close enough to New Jersey that the pressure gradient (difference) weakened and the strong winds stopped.

Strong winds affected New Jersey on the 15th.  Peak wind gusts averaged around 40 mph, with some locally higher gusts mainly in the southeast and northwest parts of the state. In southeastern New Jersey the highest wind gusts occurred during the middle of the day in the southwest flow preceding a cold front. In the rest of the state the highest wind gusts occurred following a cold frontal passage during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening on the 15th. The strong winds coupled with the rain knocked down some weak tree limbs and wires and caused isolated power outages. Peak wind gusts included 53 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 50 mph in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 47 mph in Mantoloking (Ocean County) and 45 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), Cape May (Cape May County) and the Atlantic City International Airport.  The strong gusty winds were caused by the passage of a low level jet over the southeast part of the state and then a pressure gradient (difference) between the cold front and the approaching high pressure system over the lower Ohio Valley. When the high pressure system moved closer, the strong winds ceased.

A surge of unseasonably cold air behind a departing cold front changed the rain to snow before it ended across most of New Jersey overnight on the 15th. The snow ended in most areas between Midnight EDT and 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Snowfall was an inch or less, except between 1 and 2 inches in Sussex County. In Sussex County, temperatures dropped below freezing over the higher terrain as the snow was occurring and led to slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Elsewhere, the snow ended before temperatures fell below freezing later that night.  Representative snowfall included 1.5 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 1.0 inch in Manalapan (Monmouth County) and Wantage (Sussex County), 0.8 inches in Randolph (Morris County) and 0.3 inches in Lawrence (Mercer County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County).

A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on April 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in central New Jersey. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Flooding in parts of the Passaic Basin and southeast New Jersey did not start until May began. Major creek and river flooding occurred in the Raritan and Assunpink Basins with moderate river flooding occurring in parts of the Passaic Basin. Many roadways were flooded. New Jersey Transit cross honored tickets on the commuter rail lines and buses. One hundred forty-six major roadways and interstates were flooded and completely closed or had lane closures throughout the state of New Jersey. Many roadways were re-opened by the end of the day on May 1st and most were opened again on May 2nd. The steady rain started during the afternoon of April 29th and fell at a steady light to moderate clip through the night. But, on April 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of April 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. ||The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey. But the formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very heavy rain caused roadway as well as creek and river flooding in Camden County, especially in the northern half of the county. Tidal flooding along the Delaware River exacerbated the flooding. In Brooklawn, U.S. Route 130 was flooded and closed in both directions near the Big Timber Creek. In Camden, U.S. Route 130 was also closed. In Pennsauken Township, flooding along the Cooper River closed U.S. Route 30 at the Airport Circle. U.S. Route 30 was also closed in two other locations within the township. Heavily traveled New Jersey State Route 73 was also flooded and closed within the township at Remington Avenue. ||The South Branch of the Pennsauken Creek at Cherry Hill was above its 9 foot flood stage from 554 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 556 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 10.53 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  The Cooper River at Haddonfield had minor flooding, but its 4th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  The Cooper River was above its 4.5 foot flood stage (the flood stage was revised upward) from 1023 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 206 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 4.89 feet at 1145 p.m. EDT on April 30th.||Event precipitation totals included 4.56 inches in Blackwood, 4.53 inches in Merchantville, 4.44 inches in Collingswood, 4.16 inches in Lindenwold and 4.07 inches in Sicklerville.

The strong gusty winds were caused by the passage of a low level jet over the southeast part of the state and then a pressure gradient (difference) between the cold front and the approaching high pressure system over the lower Ohio Valley. When the high pressure system moved closer, the strong winds ceased.

A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on April 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in central New Jersey. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Flooding in parts of the Passaic Basin and southeast New Jersey did not start until May began. Major creek and river flooding occurred in the Raritan and Assunpink Basins with moderate river flooding occurring in parts of the Passaic Basin. Many roadways were flooded. New Jersey Transit cross honored tickets on the commuter rail lines and buses. One hundred forty-six major roadways and interstates were flooded and completely closed or had lane closures throughout the state of New Jersey. Many roadways were re-opened by the end of the day on May 1st and most were opened again on May 2nd. The steady rain started during the afternoon of April 29th and fell at a steady light to moderate clip through the night. But, on April 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of April 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. ||The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey. But the formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Maurice River at Norma had minor flooding and was above its 4 foot flood stage from 415 p.m. EDT on May 1st through 1230 p.m. EDT on May 4th. It crested at 4.59 feet at 945 p.m. EDT on the 2nd.  Event precipitation totals included 3.45 inches at Seabrook Farms, 2.96 inches in Bridgeton, 2.94 inches in Upper Deerfield Township, 2.90 inches in Millville, 2.88 inches in Vineland and 2.77 inches in Newport.

Other major roadways that were flooded and closed included County Route 629 in Readington, Hampton Corner Road in Raritan and Hamden Road in Franklin. The Neshanic River at Reaville was above its 6.5 foot flood stage from 332  p.m. EDT on April 30th through 401 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 11.46 feet at 830 p.m. EDT on April 30th.  It was the 7th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  The South Branch of the Raritan River at Stanton had minor flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 100 a.m. EDT through 202 p.m. EDT on May 1st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof combined with an unseasonably warm air mass to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 27th. While the thunderstorms started to weaken as they approached New Jersey, their gust fronts still produced some damaging winds in the central third of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees onto wires in Springfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracking along a stalled frontal boundary extending across the central Appalachians eastward through the Mid Atlantic States produced multiple waves of heavy rain over sections of central to southern New Jersey from the late evening on the 29th through the late evening on the 30th, with some showers lasting into the morning of the 31st before low pressure tracked east and away from the coast.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation caused some poor drainage and roadway flooding.  Some streams and small rivers also overflowed their banks on the 30th and the 31st, with some not returning below flood stage until the first couple of days into April.  Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 4 inches across central to southern New Jersey.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused minor tidal flooding along the northern New Jersey coast during the evening high tide cycle on the 30th.  At Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), the highest tide reached 6.88 feet above mean lower low water at 812 p.m. EDT on the 30th.  Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from waves of heavy precipitation caused minor flooding along the Manalapan Brook from the early morning to the late afternoon of the 31st. The Manalapan Brook at Spotswood was above its 19 foot flood stage from 337 a.m. EDT through 500 p.m. EDT on the 31st, and it crested at 19.07 feet at 715 a.m. EDT. In addition, nearby roadway and poor drainage flooding occurred in a few locations. In Monroe Township, flooding closed Perrineville Road between Federal Road and Dey Grove Road. Flooding also occurred along Union Hill Road at Englishtown Road as the nearby Matchaponix Brook overflowed its banks. Event precipitation totals included 3.77 inches in North Brunswick Township, 3.55 inches in Woodbridge Township, 3.31 inches in South Brunswick Township, 3.22 inches in Edison Township, 3.04 inches in East Brunswick Township, 2.93 inches in Cranbury Township, and 2.79 inches in South Plainfield.

But, on April 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of April 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. ||The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as minor to moderate flooding in the Passaic Basin beginning on April 30th. Some of the flooding along the Passaic River did not start until May 1st. In Roxbury Township, the exit ramps in both directions off of U.S. Route 46 were flooded and closed. The heavy rain forced emergency repair lane closures on Interstate 287 in Parsippany-Troy Hills and Morristown.  Mass transit was not immune. Service was suspended along a section of New Jersey Transit's Montclair-Boonton Line until May 5th as workers repaired a track washout. Buses were made available to shuttle passengers between Montclair State University and Denville. The flooding washed away the stones that support the rails between Boonton and Towaco stations early on May 1st.  The Passaic River flooded sections of Long Hill Township and Chatham.  Some roadways remained closed into May 2nd. ||The Passaic River at Chatham had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 637 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 515 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.46 feet at 815 p.m. on April 30th. The Pequannock River at the Macopin Dam had minor flooding and was above its 5.5 foot flood stage from 1120 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 1130 a.m. EDT on May 2nd.  It crested at 6.07 feet at 155 p.m. EDT on May 1st. The Whippany River at Morristown had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 711 p.m. EDT on April 30th and 700 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.37 feet at 1100 p.m. EDT on April 30th. ||The Passaic River at Millington had minor flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 7 a.m. EDT on May 1st through 445 a.m. EDT on May 3rd. It crested at 8.24 feet at 730 p.m. EDT on May 1st. The Passaic River at Chatham had a second round of minor flooding with a slightly lower crest. The river was above its 6 foot flood stage from 130 p.m. EDT on May 1st through Noon EDT on May 4th.  It crested at 6.34 feet at 600 a.m. EDT on May 2nd. It crested at 20.23 feet at 300 p.m. EDT on May 2nd.  Lastly, the Passaic River at Two Bridges had minor flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 345 p.m. EDT on May 1st through 730 a.m. EDT on May 6th.  It crested at 10.76 feet at 330 a.m. EDT on May 3rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused considerable poor drainage flooding in Somerset County as well as major river flooding. Major flooding occurred along the Millstone and the main stem of the Raritan River, the worst flooding since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28th and 29th in 2011.  Flooding forced the closure of U.S. Route 206 in both directions south of Brooks Boulevard and south of County Route 608 or Dukes Parkway in Hillsborough Township and westbound U.S. Route 22 at Rock Avenue in  North Plainfield. The exit ramps off of Interstate 287 at southbound exit 14 were flooded and closed in Bridgewater Township. Many other roadways were flooded and closed in the county. In Franklin, Branchburg and Bridgewater Townships alone, there were 22 roads that were flooded and closed. | |The Millstone River had major flooding and the worst flooding along the river since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28th and 29th in 2011. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 543 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 130 p.m. EDT on May 3rd. It crested at 18.56 feet at 1030 a.m. EDT on May 1st, the 7th highest crest on record. Farther downstream, the Millstone River at Blackwells Mills was above its 9 foot flood stage from 945 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 115 a.m. EDT on May 3rd. It crested at 15.87 feet at Noon EDT on May 1st, also the 7th highest crest on record. Farther downstream, the Millstone River at Millstone was above its 10 foot flood stage from 1120 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 1233 a.m. EDT on May 3rd. It crested at 16.80 feet at 1145 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  Farther downstream, the Millstone River at Weston was above its 11.5 foot flood stage from 1243 a.m. EDT on May 1st through 621 a.m. EDT on May 2nd. It crested at 16.72 feet at 1000 a.m. EDT on May 1st. ||The North Branch of the Raritan River at North Branch was above its 12.3 foot flood stage from 640 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 756 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 16.10 feet at 1245 a.m. EDT on May 1st, and this was historically the 7th highest crest on record.  Farther downstream, the North Branch of the Raritan River at Raritan had minor flooding and was above its 10 foot flood stage from 831 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 1021 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 13.29 feet at 245 a.m. EDT on May 1st, the 9th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  Farther downstream, the North Branch of the Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 445 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 721 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 13.52 feet at 215 a.m. EDT on May 1st.   ||The Main Stem of the Raritan River had major flooding and its worst flooding since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. It crested at 12.22 feet at 930 a.m. EDT, the 5th highest crest on record and the highest since August 28, 2011. This was historically the 3rd highest crest on record for the creek.  At Trenton, the Assunpink Creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 604 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 437 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 11.62 feet at 345 a.m. EDT on May 1st. This was historically the 8th highest crest on record for the creek. This was historically the 5th highest crest on record for the brook and the highest crest since August 28, 2011 associated with Tropical Storm Irene. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracking along a stalled frontal boundary extending across the central Appalachians eastward through the Mid Atlantic States produced multiple waves of heavy rain over sections of central to southern New Jersey from the late evening on the 29th through the late evening on the 30th, with some showers lasting into the morning of the 31st before low pressure tracked east and away from the coast.  Run-off from waves of heavy precipitation caused some poor drainage and roadway flooding.  Some streams and small rivers also overflowed their banks on the 30th and the 31st, with some not returning below flood stage until the first couple of days into April.  Storm total rainfall measurements ranged mainly between 2 and 4 inches across central to southern New Jersey.||In addition, the combination of the heavy rain and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused minor tidal flooding along the northern New Jersey coast during the evening high tide cycle on the 30th.  At Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), the highest tide reached 6.88 feet above mean lower low water at 812 p.m. EDT on the 30th.  Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from waves of heavy precipitation caused minor flooding along the Maurice River from the evening of the 31st to the early morning of April 2nd. The Maurice River at Norma was above its 4 foot flood stage from 745 p.m. EDT on the 31st through 1230 a.m. EDT on April 2nd, and it crested at 4.06 feet at 945 a.m. EDT on April 1st. Event precipitation totals included 3.04 inches in Pittsgrove Township, 2.81 inches in Woodstown and 2.46 inches in Pennsville Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from waves of heavy precipitation caused minor to moderate flooding along the Millstone River that lasted through the night of the 31st and into the late afternoon of April 1st. The flooding forced the closure of the Griggstown Causeway and Blackwells Mills Road across the Millstone River as well as sections of Millstone River Road in Franklin and Montgomery Townships. Minor flooding occurred along the Millstone River at Blackwells Mills, and it was above its 9 foot flood stage from 710 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 900 p.m. EDT on the 31st. It crested at 9.94 feet at 845 a.m. EDT on the 31st. Moderate flooding occurred along the Millstone River at Griggstown, and it was above its 10 foot flood stage from 145 a.m. EDT on the 30th through 435 p.m. EDT on April 1st. It crested at 13.78 feet at 1145 p.m. EDT on the 30th. Minor flooding occurred along the Millstone River at Millstone, and it was above its 10 foot flood stage from 127 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 548 p.m. EDT on the 31st. It crested at 10.96 feet at 315 a.m. EDT on the 31st. Minor flooding also occurred along the Green Brook at Plainfield, and it was briefly above its 8 foot flood stage from 220 a.m. EDT through 318 a.m. EDT on the 30th. It crested at 8.05 feet at 245 a.m. EDT on the 30th. Finally, the North Branch Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 109 a.m. EDT through 1104 a.m. EDT on the 30th, and it crested at 8.80 feet at 645 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 3.48 inches in Franklin Township, 3.30 inches in Blackwells Mills, 3.28 inches in Hillsborough Township, 2.99 inches in Montgomery Township, 2.78 inches in Bridgewater Township, and 2.43 inches at the Somerville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lingered just north of the region while a cold front approached the region from the west during the day.  This resulted in a severe thunderstorm that produced wind damage in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a car at the corner of Cliff Street and 7th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across northern New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 13th.  This was in addition to the heavy rain that fell across northern New Jersey from the afternoon of the 12th into the morning of the 13th preceding the same system's warm front. Total event precipitation totals averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches. The initial shot of heavy rain left the ground nearly saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding which occurred in parts of Warren, Morris and Middlesex Counties that afternoon and early evening. ||In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide cycle on the 13th along the ocean. The high tide reached 6.30 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water. The high tides reached 7.16 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.98 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water.  The tidal influenced Watson Creek at Manasquan (Monmouth County) crested just barely above its 6 foot flood stage at 6.02 feet at 851 p.m. EDT on the 13th. While the highest tides occurred on the evening of the 13th, lesser minor tidal flooding also occurred on the evening of the 12th in Sandy Hook, the evenings of the 11th, 12th and 14th in Atlantic City and the evenings of the 11th, 12th, 14th and 15th in Cape May.  Minor tidal flooding also occurred along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 13th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.46 feet above mean lower low water early on the 14th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in Metuchen. Flooding was reported along Main Street and New Jersey State Route 27 in Metuchen.  Entire event precipitation totals included 2.00 inches in Woodbridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Creek and river flooding from the heavy rain that occurred from the evening of March 29th into the evening of March 30th persisted through the evening and night of April 1st in parts of Burlington, Gloucester, Salem and Somerset Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from waves of heavy precipitation caused minor to moderate flooding along the Millstone River that lasted through the night of March 31st and persisted at Griggstown into the late afternoon of April 1st. The flooding forced the closure of the Griggstown Causeway and Blackwells Mills Road across the Millstone River as well as sections of Millstone River Road in Franklin and Montgomery Townships. Moderate flooding occurred along the Millstone River at Griggstown, and it was above its 10 foot flood stage from 145 a.m. EDT on March 30th through 435 p.m. EDT on April 1st. It crested at 13.78 feet at 1145 p.m. EDT on March 30th.  Event precipitation totals included 3.48 inches in Franklin Township, 3.30 inches in Blackwells Mills, 3.28 inches in Hillsborough Township, 2.99 inches in Montgomery Township, 2.78 inches in Bridgewater Township, and 2.43 inches at the Somerville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on the 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in central New Jersey. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Major creek and river flooding occurred in the Raritan and Assunpink Basins with moderate river flooding occurring in parts of the Passaic Basin. Many roadways were flooded. New Jersey Transit cross honored tickets on the commuter rail lines and buses. One hundred forty-six major roadways and interstates were flooded and completely closed or had lane closures throughout the state of New Jersey.  Many roadways were re-opened by the end of the day on May 1st and most were opened again on May 2nd.  The steady rain started during the afternoon of the 29th and fell at a steady light to moderate clip through the night. But, on the 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of the 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. | |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and river and stream in Monmouth County. The flooding on the creeks and river in some locations were the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  New Jersey State Route 35 was hardest hit by the flooding as it was entirely closed in parts of Middletown, Eatontown and Hazlet Townships. New Jersey State Route 71 had lane closures in Deal.   ||The Manasquan River at Squankum had moderate flooding and was above its 7.5 foot flood stage from 1036 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 733 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 9.84 feet at 830 a.m. EDT on the 1st, the 6th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  Farther downstream, the Manasquan River at Allenwood was above its 14.5 foot flood stage from 158 a.m. EDT through 8 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 16.57 feet at 1015 a.m. EDT on May 1st, the 3rd highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  The Swimming River at Red Bank had minor flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1109 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 1010 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 7.73 feet at 315 a.m. EDT on the 1st . The Shark River at Neptune City had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1212 a.m. EDT through 500 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.18 feet at 130 a.m. EDT.||Event precipitation totals included 5.20 inches in Millstone Township, 4.93 inches in Eatontown, 4.67 inches in Red Bank, 4.58 inches in Keyport and 4.33 inches in Howell Township and Cream Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger some strong to locally severe thunderstorms across northwestern New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 13th. Hardest hit was Warren and Morris Counties. Jersey Central Power and Light reported over 1,500 of its customers lost power. Downed trees forced the suspension of travel along one commuter rail line between Warren and Morris Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs in Franklin Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through southern New Jersey during the early morning of the 19th. A couple of homes were also struck by lightning. Approximately 4,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in the Mount Holly warning and forecast area as the thunderstorms moved through. Over 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the southern half of the state. This ended the brief hot spell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused smokey conditions in the basement of a home on Birch Street in Vineland. This was one of two homes that were struck by lightning in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across central and southern New Jersey on the 17th and 18th. For many places, these were also the first 90s of the year. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s both days. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Toms River (Ocean County) and Margate (Atlantic County), 96 degrees in Cape May (Cape May County) and Somerdale (Camden County), 95 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Delran (Burlington County) and Millville (Cumberland County), 94 degrees at Berkeley Township and Cape May Courthouse (Cape May County), 93 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County), Mullica Hill (Gloucester County) and Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County) and 92 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County). A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A strong cold front with shearing winds above the surface triggered supercell type thunderstorms that occurred across extreme southern New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 22nd. Hail as large as around golf ball size caused crop, siding and automobile damage with Cumberland County the hardest hit. No serious injuries were reported. The large, damaging hail was the tipping point for the U.S. Department of Agriculture to designate six southern New Jersey counties as Secretarial Disaster areas. Cumberland, Gloucester and Salem Counties were the primary counties with Atlantic, Cape May and Camden Counties also eligible for assistance. Hail crop damage to one farm in Cumberland County alone was estimated at one million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped penny size hail in Mullica Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very heavy rain caused roadway as well as creek and river flooding in Camden County, especially in the northern half of the county. Tidal flooding along the Delaware River exacerbated the flooding. In Brooklawn, U.S. Route 130 was flooded and closed in both directions near the Big Timber Creek. In Camden, U.S. Route 130 was also closed. In Pennsauken Township, flooding along the Cooper River closed U.S. Route 30 at the Airport Circle. U.S. Route 30 was also closed in two other locations within the township. Heavily traveled New Jersey State Route 73 was also flooded and closed within the township at Remington Avenue. ||The South Branch of the Pennsauken Creek at Cherry Hill was above its 9 foot flood stage from 554 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 556 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 10.53 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  The Cooper River at Haddonfield had minor flooding, but its 4th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  The Cooper River was above its 4.5 foot flood stage (the flood stage was revised upward) from 1023 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 206 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 4.89 feet at 1145 p.m. EDT on April 30th.||Event precipitation totals included 4.56 inches in Blackwood, 4.53 inches in Merchantville, 4.44 inches in Collingswood, 4.16 inches in Lindenwold and 4.07 inches in Sicklerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the freshwater runoff, onshore flow and high astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the overnight high tide cycle on April 30th and May 1st. In Camden County, tidal flooding extended through the Airport Circle along the Cooper River to Pennsauken Township. The Admiral Wilson Boulevard was also flooded along the Cooper River. The flooding damaged the ramps to the county boathouse in Pennsauken. In addition, U.S. Route 130 in Brooklawn was flooded near the Big Timber Creek. Vehicles were stuck in the flood waters. In Salem County, tidal flooding forced the closure of U.S. Route 130 over the Oldman's Creek. The onshore flow that started on April 29th caused minor tidal flooding in the Delaware River with both high tide cycles on April 30th. But, the combination of the residual onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the high tide cycle overnight on April 30th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water at 306 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record along the Delaware River in Philadelphia. Farther south, the highest tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County) reached 8.25 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.||The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the moderate coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River.
A lee side trough and adequate surface heating triggered scattered strong to severe thunderstorms into portions of northern to central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Liberty Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough and adequate surface heating triggered scattered strong to severe thunderstorms into portions of northern to central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and power poles in several locations in Basking Ridge, resulting in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across northern New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 13th.  This was in addition to the heavy rain that fell across northern New Jersey from the afternoon of the 12th into the morning of the 13th preceding the same system's warm front. Total event precipitation totals averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches. The initial shot of heavy rain left the ground nearly saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding which occurred in parts of Warren, Morris and Middlesex Counties that afternoon and early evening. ||In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide cycle on the 13th along the ocean. The high tide reached 6.30 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water. The high tides reached 7.16 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.98 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water.  The tidal influenced Watson Creek at Manasquan (Monmouth County) crested just barely above its 6 foot flood stage at 6.02 feet at 851 p.m. EDT on the 13th. While the highest tides occurred on the evening of the 13th, lesser minor tidal flooding also occurred on the evening of the 12th in Sandy Hook, the evenings of the 11th, 12th and 14th in Atlantic City and the evenings of the 11th, 12th, 14th and 15th in Cape May.  Minor tidal flooding also occurred along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 13th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.46 feet above mean lower low water early on the 14th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in southwest Warren County centered in and around Phillipsburg as well as Mansfield and Franklin Townships. Road closures were reported in both municipalities. Entire event precipitation totals included 3.52 inches in Mansfield Township, 3.29 inches in Greenwich Township, 3.11 inches in Stewartsville, 2.88 inches in Washington Township and 2.57 inches in Hope.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and large tree limbs in Mansfield Township. Jersey Central Power and Light reported about 1,500 homes and businesses lost power in Warren County, mainly in Mansfield Township.

A wildfire consumed approximately 1,600 acres of forest in Wharton State Forest in Washington Township (Burlington County) until it was contained.  The wildfire started on the afternoon of the 6th. A wind shift and increase in overall wind speed associated with the passage of a sea breeze front around 5 p.m. EDT that afternoon helped spread the fire faster. Aviation resources were used to help battle the blaze as the fire was located in an extremely remote area of the state forest and resources were having trouble accessing the fire area due to poor road conditions. The wildfire was about 30 percent contained at 930 p.m. EDT on the 6th.  Due to the wind shift from the sea breeze front and then the overall synoptic scale easterly flow, the smoke from the wildfire could be smelled throughout Burlington and Ocean Counties. Cooler and moister conditions coupled with afternoon rain on the 7th helped the New Jersey Forest Fire Service achieve 100 percent containment of the wildfire at 4 p.m. EDT that afternoon.||At the opposite end of the state, a few smaller wildfires occurred in Sussex County from the 6th into the night of the 7th. The largest was a 75 acre wildfire on a mountain ridge behind an A&P Supermarket on County Route 515 in Vernon Township. Forty firefighters battled the blaze and water from Highland Lake was used to extinguish it. Smaller brush fires charred 9 acres along U.S. Route 206 in Byram Township. Other brush fires occurred along State Route 15 in Sparta Township and Twin Oaks Road in Stillwater Township.

The onshore flow that started on April 29th caused minor tidal flooding in the Delaware River with both high tide cycles on April 30th. But, the combination of the residual onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the high tide cycle overnight on April 30th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water at 306 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record along the Delaware River in Philadelphia. Farther south, the highest tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County) reached 8.25 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.||The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the moderate coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River.
A lee side trough and adequate surface heating triggered scattered strong to severe thunderstorms into portions of northern to central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced some tree damage which took down wires in Delanco.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced some tree damage and took down wires in Delran.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with shearing winds above the surface triggered supercell type thunderstorms that occurred across extreme southern New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 22nd. Hail as large as around golf ball size caused crop, siding and automobile damage with Cumberland County the hardest hit. No serious injuries were reported. The large, damaging hail was the tipping point for the U.S. Department of Agriculture to designate six southern New Jersey counties as Secretarial Disaster areas. Cumberland, Gloucester and Salem Counties were the primary counties with Atlantic, Cape May and Camden Counties also eligible for assistance. Hail crop damage to one farm in Cumberland County alone was estimated at one million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail that covered roadways in the Rosenhayn section of Deerfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire consumed approximately 1,600 acres of forest in Wharton State Forest in Washington Township (Burlington County) until it was contained.  The wildfire started on the afternoon of the 6th. A wind shift and increase in overall wind speed associated with the passage of a sea breeze front around 5 p.m. EDT that afternoon helped spread the fire faster. Aviation resources were used to help battle the blaze as the fire was located in an extremely remote area of the state forest and resources were having trouble accessing the fire area due to poor road conditions. The wildfire was about 30 percent contained at 930 p.m. EDT on the 6th.  Due to the wind shift from the sea breeze front and then the overall synoptic scale easterly flow, the smoke from the wildfire could be smelled throughout Burlington and Ocean Counties. Cooler and moister conditions coupled with afternoon rain on the 7th helped the New Jersey Forest Fire Service achieve 100 percent containment of the wildfire at 4 p.m. EDT that afternoon.||At the opposite end of the state, a few smaller wildfires occurred in Sussex County from the 6th into the night of the 7th. The largest was a 75 acre wildfire on a mountain ridge behind an A&P Supermarket on County Route 515 in Vernon Township. Forty firefighters battled the blaze and water from Highland Lake was used to extinguish it. Smaller brush fires charred 9 acres along U.S. Route 206 in Byram Township. Other brush fires occurred along State Route 15 in Sparta Township and Twin Oaks Road in Stillwater Township.

Downed trees forced the suspension of travel along one commuter rail line between Warren and Morris Counties.
