EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dropped around an inch of precipitation in east central and southern New Jersey and between 1 and 2.5 inches of precipitation from the Raritan Basin northward in the rest of the state on the 15th. While some urban and poor drainage flooding occurred, because of the recent dry weather only isolated river flooding was reported. The steady rain began during the late morning and early afternoon of the 15th and fell heavy at times during the mid and late afternoon. The precipitation ended shortly after midnight EDT on the 16th. Precipitation ended as snow with the most accumulation over the higher terrain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Branch of the Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 937 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 335 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 7.62 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 16th.  Event precipitation totals included 1.93 inches in Peapack-Gladstone, 1.45 inches in Bernards Township, 1.36 inches in Bridgewater Township, 1.31 inches in Hillsborough Township, 1.30 inches in Raritan, 1.25 inches in Bedminster Township and 1.10 inches in Somerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong gusty northwest winds, the lack of significant rain in over a week and low relative humidity levels helped several major wildfires spread quickly across the southern half of New Jersey on the 23rd and 24th. Containment was not reached until the 26th. Residents were evacuated in one of the wildfires in Berkeley Township (Ocean County), but property damage was minimal and no serious injuries were reported.  The dry conditions led to a ban of all outdoor fires on the 24th in New Jersey.||The first major wildfire occurred on the 23rd in Cumberland County in the Edward G. Bevan Fish and Wildlife Management Area in Downe Township between Spring Garden and Shaw Mills Road. The New Jersey Forest Fire Service was dispatched on the afternoon of the 23rd and closed Dividing Creek-Millville Road from Battle Lane to Railroad Avenue and Ackley Road from Dividing Creek-Millville Road to Shaws Mill Road  to protect residents and stage fire equipment. Fifty firefighters from the New Jersey Forest Fire Service, along with area fire departments, spent the afternoon and evening of the 23rd trying to contain the fire���s spread. Along with fire apparatuses, the New Jersey Forest Fire Service used tractors, plows, an air tanker and an observational helicopter to help contain the wildfire. The wildfire was considered 85 percent contained as of 5 p.m. EDT on the 23rd. The wildfire was declared under control on the morning of the 25th and charred 1,535 acres.||A pair of wildfires occurred in Berkeley Township and Beachwood Borough.  The higher impact wildfire occurred along the Berkeley Township and Beachwood Borough border.  It started at 1045 a.m. EDT on the 24th near Beachwood in Berkeley Township.  About 400 to 600 residents between Northern Boulevard and Maryland Avenue in Berkeley Township were evacuated. Children from the Intermediate South Elementary School in Beachwood were sent home early as the school was used as a command post. About 15 fire companies were battling the blaze. Three bulldozers, several helicopters and firefighting planes were also used to battle the blaze. The wildfire was declared 100 percent contained at 10 p.m. EDT on the 25th and evacuees were permitted to return home. The wildfire charred about 307 acres. Only minor structural damage was reported. One shed and one swing set were burned and some singeing of homes occurred.  No serious injuries were reported.  The second wildfire occurred in Double Trouble State Park in the southwestern part of Berkeley Township off of Pinewald-Keswick Road. The wildfire started at 5 pm. EDT on the 24th. It was 100 percent contained on the 26th at Noon EDT and consumed 220 acres.  |  |The last major wildfire started during the afternoon of the 24th in Franklin Township in Gloucester County between Whitehall and Bluebell Roads. As of 930 a.m. EDT on the 25th, the wildfire was 60 percent contained. There were no reported serious injuries, no structures were damaged and no roads needed to be closed. However, heavy smoke was in the area. Lighter southeast winds and rain late in the day and overnight on the 25th helped firefighters get 100 percent containment on the morning of the 26th. The wildfire burned 569 acres.||Smaller wildfires occurred in Whiting and Jackson in Ocean County as well as in Mercer and Atlantic Counties. Gusty northwest winds on both the 23rd and 24th made it extremely difficult for firefighters to battle the wildfires. Peak wind gusts at the Atlantic City International Airport were 41 mph and 36 mph respectively.

A major brush fire on the Edison and Woodbridge municipal line (Middlesex County) caused traffic disruptions on the 10th. The fire was first reported about 11 a.m. EDT that morning between Olympic Drive and the Black Ditch by the Raritan Center Industrial Park. Dozens of firefighters as well as aircraft battled the blaze. A huge column of black smoke was seen from miles away. Smoke from the fire was smelled in Sussex and Warren Counties and also on Staten Island, New York. It was seen as far south as Lakewood in Ocean County. Several local roadways were closed and or detoured. The south winds blew smoke over the FedEx facility on Riverside Drive.  The smoke was also seen from major roadways that converge in the area and included the New Jersey Turnpike, the Garden State Parkway, United States Routes 1 and 9 and New Jersey State Route 440.  Some warehouses and businesses were potentially threatened, however, no damage or evacuations occurred.  While the wetlands in the area prevented access, they also stopped the fire from spreading faster. At 3 p.m. EDT on the 10th, the wildfire was about 50 percent contained and at 520 p.m. EDT on the 10th, it was 100 percent contained. Approximately 194 acres of brush were consumed.  No property damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across central and southern New Jersey on the 17th and 18th. For many places, these were also the first 90s of the year. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s both days. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Toms River (Ocean County) and Margate (Atlantic County), 96 degrees in Cape May (Cape May County) and Somerdale (Camden County), 95 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Delran (Burlington County) and Millville (Cumberland County), 94 degrees at Berkeley Township and Cape May Courthouse (Cape May County), 93 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County), Mullica Hill (Gloucester County) and Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County) and 92 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County). A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A wildfire charred nearly 1,000 acres of brush in Wharton State Forest in Waterford Township in Camden County on the 26th through the 28th. The wildfire started during the evening on the 26th and occurred west of U.S. Route 206, south of Atsion Road and north of Chew Road near the Camden and Burlington County line in Waterford Township. Gusty west to northwest winds (Peak wind gust of 30 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport) on the 27th pushed the smoke plume through Atlantic County and passed it over Atlantic City. Heavy smoke was reported in the area of the wildfire, but the wildfire posed no threat to residences or structures. At 9 a.m. EDT on the 28th, the fire was 80 percent contained. The wildfire was 100 percent contained at 3 p.m. EDT on the 28th and burned approximately 907 acres. Lighter winds and in the opposite direction of the 27th helped firefighters make large strides toward containment.||A second, separate wildfire occurred in the Edward G Bevan Fish and Wildlife Management Area in Downe Township in Cumberland County. This wildfire started on the afternoon of the 27th and was contained at 10 a.m. EDT on the 28th. About 80 acres were consumed. Through the end of April, New Jersey Forestry service logged 488 wildfires which burned 6,226 acres in 2014. By comparison to date in 2013, there were 464 wildfires which burned 750 acres.

A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through southern New Jersey during the early morning of the 19th. A couple of homes were also struck by lightning. Approximately 4,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in the Mount Holly warning and forecast area as the thunderstorms moved through. Over 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the southern half of the state. This ended the brief hot spell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused another house fire in Gloucester County on Hickory Lane in Harrison Township. No serious injuries were reported.

A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on the 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in central New Jersey. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Major creek and river flooding occurred in the Raritan and Assunpink Basins with moderate river flooding occurring in parts of the Passaic Basin. Many roadways were flooded. New Jersey Transit cross honored tickets on the commuter rail lines and buses. One hundred forty-six major roadways and interstates were flooded and completely closed or had lane closures throughout the state of New Jersey.  Many roadways were re-opened by the end of the day on May 1st and most were opened again on May 2nd.  The steady rain started during the afternoon of the 29th and fell at a steady light to moderate clip through the night. But, on the 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of the 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. | |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused considerable poor drainage and creek flooding in the northern two-thirds of Burlington County. Municipalities along the branches of the Rancocas Creek were hardest hit by the flooding, in particular Lumberton and Mount Holly.  County Route 541 through Lumberton was closed through the morning of May 2nd because of flooding along the South Branch of the Rancocas Creek. Residents from 30 homes in Lumberton Township were evacuated. Four to five homes in Lumberton suffered damage onto the first floor. Most of the other home damage in the township involved flooded basements or damaged vehicles. Along the North Branch of the Rancocas Creek, the Mount Holly Municipal Building was flooded, and the township���s Buttonwood Dam was badly damaged. Floodwaters along the creek were lapping at homes in Eastampton Township. Residents from several homes in Medford near the Southwest Branch of the Rancocas Creek were also evacuated. All families in the county displaced by flooding were able to return to their homes. |  |Major roadways that were flooded and closed included  New Jersey State Route 73 in Maple Shade Township and New Jersey State Route 70 in Medford Township. Major roadway partial closures included  New Jersey State Route 38 in Moorestown and Mount Laurel Townships, New Jersey State Route 73 in Palmyra Township, U.S. Route 130 in Burlington Township and U.S. Route 206 in Bordentown Township.  | |The North Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Pemberton had minor flooding and was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 750 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 430 p.m. EDT on May 3rd. It crested at 2.98 feet at 1100 p.m. EDT on May 1st.  Farther downstream, a new gage along the North Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Iron Work Park was above its 11.7 foot flood stage from 1135 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 905 a.m. EDT on the 2nd.  It crested at 13.80 feet at 815 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  The South Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Vincentown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1007 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 756 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 8.60 feet at 530 a.m. EDT on the 1st, the 4th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  The Southwest Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Medford was above its 12 foot flood stage from 922 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 1116 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 14.38 feet at 230 a.m. EDT on the 1st, the 4th highest crest on record and the highest since the nor���easter on April 16, 2007.  The South Branch of the Pennsauken Creek at Cherry Hill was above its 9 foot flood stage from 554 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 556 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 10.53 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on May 1st. ||The Greenwood Branch at New Lisbon had minor flooding and was above its 5 foot flood stage from 748 a.m. EDT on the 1st through 245 a.m. EDT on the 3rd. It crested at 6.25 feet at 1045 p.m. EDT on the 1st. The Crosswicks Creek at Extonville was above its 12 foot flood stage from 146 a.m. EDT through 857 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 14.30 feet at 845 a.m. EDT on the 1st. This was the 3rd highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.||Event precipitation totals included 6.32 inches in Riverton, 5.44 inches in Medford Township, 5.28 inches in Crosswicks, 5.23 inches in Moorestown, 5.14 inches in Mount Laurel and 4.96 inches at the Mount Holly WFO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire consumed about 250 acres of forest in the Wharton State Forest in Shamong Township off of Stokes Road (County Route 541) on the 25th. The wildfire was first spotted at 930 a.m. EDT that morning and was contained at Noon EDT on the 26th. Overall lighter southeast winds which also raised humidity levels and overnight rain helped with containment efforts. No evacuations were necessary.

A low pressure system developed along the northern Gulf Coast on February 12th, and moved to near Cape Hatteras and the Virginia capes by the morning of the 13th. The intensifying low then headed northeast, passing east of Long Island and into the Gulf of Maine early the morning of the 14th, bringing heavy snow and ice to northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported widespread snowfall of 13 to 16 inches. Freezing rain accretion of 1 to 3 tenths of an inch also occurred per observations from surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on the 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in central New Jersey. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Major creek and river flooding occurred in the Raritan and Assunpink Basins with moderate river flooding occurring in parts of the Passaic Basin. Many roadways were flooded. New Jersey Transit cross honored tickets on the commuter rail lines and buses. One hundred forty-six major roadways and interstates were flooded and completely closed or had lane closures throughout the state of New Jersey.  Many roadways were re-opened by the end of the day on May 1st and most were opened again on May 2nd.  The steady rain started during the afternoon of the 29th and fell at a steady light to moderate clip through the night. But, on the 30th the rain fell heavy at times during the morning and afternoon in the southern third of the state, and from the late morning into the evening of the 30th elsewhere in the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. | |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused considerable roadway flooding in Middlesex County and also caused some brook flooding within the county. Flooding also occurred along the Raritan River. A few of the crests were the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. Nearly every major roadway in the county had flooding and many of them were closed. U.S. Route 1 was closed in both directions in South Brunswick Township.  New Jersey State Route 28 was closed in two locations in Middlesex Borough.  New Jersey State Route 33 was closed in Monroe Township. New Jersey State Route 18 was closed in New Brunswick. New Jersey State Route 171 was closed in North Brunswick and a sink hole also formed. The heavy rain forced emergency repairs on a northbound lane of the Garden State Parkway in Woodbridge.||The Lawrence Brook at Weston Mills was above its 18 foot flood stage from 705 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 830 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 18.88 feet at 130 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  The Bound Brook at Middlesex had minor flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1010 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 923 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 10.40 feet at 145 a.m. EDT on the 1st, the 6th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. The Manalapan Brook at Spotswood was above its 19 foot flood stage from 436 a.m. EDT on May 1st through 407 p.m. EDT on May 2nd. It crested at 20.31 feet at 500 p.m. EDT on the 1st, the 3rd highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.||Event precipitation totals included 5.59 inches in Matawan, 5.22 inches in Edison Township, 4.97 inches in North Brunswick Township, 4.96 inches in Colonia and 4.92 inches in East Brunswick Township and Woodbridge Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of thunderstorms preceding a cold front moved through the Philadelphia metropolitan area suburbs before it dissipated. It caused isolated wind damage in Camden County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Collingswood. One tree fell onto and damaged the porch of one home. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an onshore flow and higher than normal astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused widespread minor tidal flooding along the New Jersey coast during the evening high tide cycle on the 29th and sporadic minor tidal flooding with the two subsequent high tides. At the Atlantic City tide gage (Atlantic County), the highest tide reached 6.64 feet above mean lower low water at 848 p.m. EDT on the 29th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water. At the Sandy Hook tide gage (Monmouth County), the highest tide reached 7.68 feet above mean lower low water at 924 p.m. EDT on the 29th. It just missed the moderate tidal flooding threshold level which is 7.70 feet above mean lower low water. At the Cape May Ferry tide gage (Cape May County), the highest tide reached 7.58 feet above mean lower low water at 817 p.m. EDT on the 29th. It just missed the moderate tidal flooding threshold level which is 7.70 feet above mean lower low water. The Watson Creek at Manasquan (Monmouth County) was above its 6 foot flood stage from 757 p.m. EDT through 1012 p.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 6.49 feet at 906 p.m. EDT on the 29th.||Tidal flooding was not restricted to the ocean front. The combination of freshwater runoff, onshore flow and high astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the overnight high tide cycle on the 30th. In Camden County, tidal flooding extended through the Airport Circle along the Cooper River to Pennsauken Township. The Admiral Wilson Boulevard was also flooded along the Cooper River. The flooding damaged the ramps to the county boathouse in Pennsauken. In addition, U.S. Route 130 in Brooklawn was flooded near the Big Timber Creek. Vehicles were stuck in the flood waters. In Salem County, tidal flooding forced the closure of U.S. Route 130 over the Oldman's Creek. The onshore flow that started on the 29th caused minor tidal flooding in the Delaware River with both high tide cycles on the 30th. But, the combination of the residual onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the high tide cycle overnight on the 30th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water at 306 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record along the Delaware River in Philadelphia. Farther south, the highest tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County) reached 8.25 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.||The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the moderate coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River.

The intensifying low then headed northeast, passing east of Long Island and into the Gulf of Maine early the morning of the 14th, bringing heavy snow and ice to northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newark Airport reported snowfall of 11.9 inches. Freezing rain accretion of 1 to 3 tenths of an inch also occurred per observations from surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough and an unstable air mass produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey during the late afternoon to evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Stillwater Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough and an unstable air mass produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey during the late afternoon to evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in and around Blairstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough and an unstable air mass produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey during the late afternoon to evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees along Tamarack Drive and Hawthorn Drive in Roxbury Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across northern New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 13th.  This was in addition to the heavy rain that fell across northern New Jersey from the afternoon of the 12th into the morning of the 13th preceding the same system's warm front. Total event precipitation totals averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches. The initial shot of heavy rain left the ground nearly saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding which occurred in parts of Warren, Morris and Middlesex Counties that afternoon and early evening. ||In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the evening high tide cycle on the 13th along the ocean. The high tide reached 6.30 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water. The high tides reached 7.16 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.98 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water.  The tidal influenced Watson Creek at Manasquan (Monmouth County) crested just barely above its 6 foot flood stage at 6.02 feet at 851 p.m. EDT on the 13th. While the highest tides occurred on the evening of the 13th, lesser minor tidal flooding also occurred on the evening of the 12th in Sandy Hook, the evenings of the 11th, 12th and 14th in Atlantic City and the evenings of the 11th, 12th, 14th and 15th in Cape May.  Minor tidal flooding also occurred along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 13th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.46 feet above mean lower low water early on the 14th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flooding in East Hanover Township. New Jersey State Route 10 was closed within the township with some underpasses totally submerged. Entire event precipitation totals included 2.41 inches in Denville, 2.28 inches in Mendham Township and 2.11 inches in Mine Hill Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across central and southern New Jersey on the 17th and 18th. For many places, these were also the first 90s of the year. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s both days. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Toms River (Ocean County) and Margate (Atlantic County), 96 degrees in Cape May (Cape May County) and Somerdale (Camden County), 95 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Delran (Burlington County) and Millville (Cumberland County), 94 degrees at Berkeley Township and Cape May Courthouse (Cape May County), 93 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County), Mullica Hill (Gloucester County) and Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County) and 92 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County). A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A 14-year-old boy drowned after getting caught in a rip current while swimming with his friends off of Ocean City. (Cape May County).  It occurred shortly before 7 p.m. EDT on the 29th.  The Ocean City Beach Patrol rescued six to eight people who were swimming with the teenager near the 9th Street Beach when they were swept offshore by the rip current.

A 25-year-old male drowned after getting caught in a rip current in Island Beach State Park (Ocean County) just before Noon EDT on the 18th.  The person was a first-time swimmer on the New Jersey coast and was swimming on an unguarded beach with a group of friends. One friend tried to help him, but lost control of him.  His body was found less than 24 hours later about one mile from where he went missing.

A lightning strike from a thunderstorm caused a condominium fire in Mount Laurel Township (Burlington County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire left several people displaced from their homes in a condominium complex on Friday night on Tarnbrook Drive in Mount Laurel Township.  Eight units were declared uninhabitable after the blaze, which was controlled in less than an hour. Four nearby townships assisted in battling the blaze. Lightning struck the upper part of the building and started a roof fire.  Other condominium units suffered fire, smoke and water damage.

Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Warren County as well as some river flooding in the county. The Pequest River at Pequest was above its 4 foot flood stage from 627 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 200 a.m. EDT on the 3rd. It crested at 4.60 feet at 330 a.m.  EDT on May 1st . It was the 9th highest crest on record and the highest since the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee on September 8, 2011.  The Paulins Kill at Blairstown was above its 5 foot flood stage from 100 a.m. EDT through 515 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  It crested at 6.10 feet at 1030 a.m. EDT on May 1st .||Event precipitation totals included 4.80 inches in Washington, 4.61 inches in White Township, 4.11 inches in Blairstown Township, 4.08 inches in Mansfield Township and 4.06 inches in Broadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across New Jersey centered on the 30th. This caused widespread poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers throughout most of the state. Rainfall amounts of 2 inches in six hours were common. The rain ended from the south to north from around Midnight EDT to around 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. | |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. Farther downstream, the Middle Brook at Bound Brook was above its 11 foot flood stage from 520 a.m. EDT through 230 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 12.22 feet at 930 a.m. EDT, the 5th highest crest on record and the highest since August 28, 2011.  The Green Brook at Plainfield was above its 8 foot flood stage from 508 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 327 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 9.92 feet at 9 p.m. EDT on the 30th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with a large cutoff low pressure system over the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes region caused periods of heavy rain, which resulted in continued flooding across portions of Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Voluntary evacuations continued from April 30 in Cranford due to flooding on the Rahway River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across central and southern New Jersey on the 17th and 18th. For many places, these were also the first 90s of the year. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s both days. Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Toms River (Ocean County) and Margate (Atlantic County), 96 degrees in Cape May (Cape May County) and Somerdale (Camden County), 95 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Delran (Burlington County) and Millville (Cumberland County), 94 degrees at Berkeley Township and Cape May Courthouse (Cape May County), 93 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County), Mullica Hill (Gloucester County) and Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County) and 92 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County). A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A strong cold front with shearing winds above the surface triggered supercell type thunderstorms that occurred across extreme southern New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 22nd. Hail as large as around golf ball size caused crop, siding and automobile damage with Cumberland County the hardest hit. No serious injuries were reported. The large, damaging hail was the tipping point for the U.S. Department of Agriculture to designate six southern New Jersey counties as Secretarial Disaster areas. Cumberland, Gloucester and Salem Counties were the primary counties with Atlantic, Cape May and Camden Counties also eligible for assistance. Hail crop damage to one farm in Cumberland County alone was estimated at one million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped golf ball size hail in Woolwich Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding as well as minor to moderate flooding in the Passaic Basin. Some of the flooding along the Passaic River did not start until May 1st. That flood information is included below and will be repeated in May's stormdata entry. In Roxbury Township, the exit ramps in both directions off of U.S. Route 46 were flooded and closed. The heavy rain forced emergency repair lane closures on Interstate 287 in Parsippany-Troy Hills and Morristown.  Mass transit was not immune. Service was suspended along a section of New Jersey Transit's Montclair-Boonton Line until May 5th as workers repaired a track washout. Buses were made available to shuttle passengers between Montclair State University and Denville. The flooding washed away the stones that support the rails between Boonton and Towaco stations early on May 1st.  The Passaic River flooded sections of Long Hill Township and Chatham.  Some roadways remained closed into May 2nd. ||The Passaic River at Chatham had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 637 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 515 a.m. EDT on May 1st. The Pequannock River at the Macopin Dam had minor flooding and was above its 5.5 foot flood stage from 1120 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 1130 a.m. EDT on May 2nd.  It crested at 6.07 feet at 155 p.m. EDT on May 1st. The Whippany River at Morristown had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 711 p.m. EDT on the 30th and 700 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.37 feet at 1100 p.m. EDT on April 30th. ||In May, the Passaic River at Millington had minor flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 7 a.m. EDT on May 1st through 445 a.m. EDT on May 3rd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary focused slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain in southwestern New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening of the 10th. This caused poor drainage flash flooding as well as flash flooding of some smaller creeks. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 2 to 4 inches in sections of Salem and Gloucester Counties and nearly 2 inches in Camden County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused flooding along the Mantua Creek near Pitman that continued into the middle of the evening on the 10th.  The Mantua Creek near Pitman was above its 5 foot flood stage from 627 p.m. EDT through 854 p.m. EDT on the 10th. It crested at 5.32 feet at 730 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals in Gloucester County included 4.11 inches in Pitman, 2.69 in Monroe Township, 2.59 inches in Washington Township, 2.20 inches in Sewell and 2.04 inches in Sewell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks and roadways in Pilesgrove Township just to the northwest of Woodstown. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3.0 and 3.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through southern New Jersey during the early morning of the 19th. A couple of homes were also struck by lightning. Approximately 4,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in the Mount Holly warning and forecast area as the thunderstorms moved through. Over 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the southern half of the state. This ended the brief hot spell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to a chimney caused an attic fire in a home along Lynn Drive in West Deptford Township. Fire fighters were able to bring the fire under control within 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure slowly worked its way through the region, which caused isolated severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and flash flooding in portions of Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported just west of Bloomfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching lee side trof coupled with a hot and humid air mass triggered a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms that affected the northern two thirds of New Jersey during the late evening of the 13th. The thunderstorms progressively became weaker as they moved into and traveled through New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires across the northern half of Hunterdon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a relatively moist air mass, light winds preceding a warm front and snow cover produced widespread dense fog in southern New Jersey during the morning and in northern New Jersey during the morning and afternoon of the 21st.  The fog contributed to a fatal traffic three vehicle accident involving a school van in Upper Pittsgrove Township in Salem County.  The three vehicles collided near the intersection of U.S. Route 40 and Burlington Road.  A tractor-trailer was traveling west on U.S. Route 40 and a school van was traveling east on the road and followed by another vehicle.  A fourth vehicle was also traveling east on U.S. Route 40 in front of the school van. The fourth vehicle went to make a left turn onto Burlington Road and the tractor-trailer swerved into the eastbound lane to avoid striking the vehicle and in the process collided with the school van. The school van was then rear-ended by the vehicle following it. The 58-year-old woman driver of the school van was killed. Both the tractor-trailer and the other vehicle's drivers suffered non life threatening injuries as did a 17-year-old boy on the school van. The dense fog lifted and dispersed in the southern part of the state as a warm front moved through, but the northern part of the state was foggy until a cold front moved through the state during the afternoon.

A period of freezing rain affected most of New Jersey (except the southeast part of the state) on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways.  Numerous accidents were reported along Interstate 78 in northern New Jersey. Ice accumulations averaged around one tenth of an inch with the highest amounts in and around Mercer County. Precipitation started briefly as sleet before transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually plain rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in the Philadelphia suburbs of southwest New Jersey. The freezing rain occurred from the late morning through the early evening of the 5th in central New Jersey and the Raritan Basin. In northwest New Jersey, the freezing rain occurred from the afternoon into the evening of the 5th. In all areas, precipitation changed to plain rain before ending.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Hopewell Township (Mercer County) and around one-twentieth of an inch (0.05) in Lumberton (Burlington County) and Somerville (Somerset County). The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States.  While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

A winter weather event dropped snow across northwest New Jersey and the Raritan Valley and a wintry mix that became predominately freezing rain across the rest of central Jersey as well as southwest New Jersey on the 10th. This caused hundreds and hundreds of accidents, a couple of which were fatal, during the morning commute, especially in the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan area suburbs and Ocean County. Snowfall in northwest New Jersey and the Raritan Valley averaged 1 to 2 inches. But it was the around one tenth of an inch of ice that fell across southwest New Jersey and the rest of central New Jersey that had the greatest impact.||In the Raritan Basin and northwest New Jersey precipitation started as snow between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 10th. Precipitation changed to sleet before it ended in the Raritan Basin. Precipitation ended between 11 a.m. EST and Noon EST that morning. In central New Jersey and the local Philadelphia suburbs in southwest New Jersey, precipitation started briefly as snow between 5 a.m. and 7 a.m EST, but changed quickly to sleet and then freezing rain by 8 a.m. EST. The freezing rain changed to plain rain between 10 a.m. EST and Noon EST that morning and ended early that afternoon.||In Ocean County, in Brick Township, a 76-year-old woman was killed after she left her car and was then struck by another vehicle during part of a thirteen vehicle crash on the Beaver Dam Bridge connecting the township with Point Pleasant. Vehicles could not stop after the initial accident occurred and one struck struck the woman. Three other persons suffered injuries. The second fatal accident occurred in Camden County.  A 29-year-old woman was killed after her vehicle crashed into a Philadelphia-bound New Jersey Transit bus on West Branch Avenue in Pine Hill. Her Sports Utility Vehicle was approaching the bus from the other side of the road when it spun out of control and struck the front of the bus. The driver was killed and a passenger was seriously injured.  Seventeen of the twenty-one passengers on the bus were treated for non-life-threatening injuries.  ||New Jersey State Police responded to over 250 calls of accidents on major roadways statewide just between 7 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST.  Camden County emergency personnel responded to 1000 calls, 71 of them involving accidents.  The Virtua Hospital system treated 225 people alone for slip and fall accidents in Burlington and Camden Counties. New Jersey Transit buses were delayed by up to two hours in Burlington, Camden and Gloucester Counties. The Walt Whitman Bridge connecting Camden County to Philadelphia was closed for an hour after a multi-vehicular accident and a need to salt the entire span. School buses stopped running their routes because it became too dangerous.  Meals on Wheels stopped their deliveries. ||In Gloucester County, there were a couple of school bus accidents in Washington Township, none involving children. A fire truck also ran off the road in the township.  In Camden County in Gloucester Township, a private ambulance overturned. A police vehicle was struck by another vehicle after it stopped to investigate another accident. In Burlington County, in Southampton Township, a fire truck spun out on U.S. Route 206 and struck four other vehicles. A firefighter was ejected from the vehicle and seriously injured. In Mercer County, a ten vehicle accident occurred on U.S. Route 1 in Lawrence Township and closed the northbound lanes for hours. Eastbound Interstate 195 was closed between Robbinsville in Mercer County and Upper Freehold in Monmouth County due to icing. In Ocean County, there were 30 vehicle accidents in Brick Township. In Manchester Township, there were 21 crashes within three hours, four with injuries. In Barnegat Township, there were 25 vehicle crashes (in 90 minutes), 14 slip and fall incidents and 16 injuries overall.  In Middlesex County, in South Brunswick there were multiple car crashes and the township advised motorists to avoid the area as many roads in the eastern part of the township were impassable.   ||Representative ice accumulation included 0.15 inches in Hamilton Township (Mercer County). Representative snowfall included 1.5 inches in Pittstown (Hunterdon County), 1.4 inches in Phillipsburg (Warren County), 1.3 inches in Bridgewater (Somerset County), 1.2 inches in Sussex (Sussex County), 0.7 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 0.5 inches in Succasanna (Morris County) and 0.4 inches in Hamilton Township (Mercer County).| |The wintry mix was caused by the combination of an approaching warm front and the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean from the departing arctic high. Extremely cold weather preceding this event made it easy for the rain to freeze on contact, even in places where air temperatures were slightly above freezing.

A cold front coupled with an upper air disturbance triggered bands of accumulating snow showers across the Raritan Basin and Northwest New Jersey on the morning of the 18th. Snowfall averaged one to three inches in Sussex and Warren Counties and around one inch. In Morris, Hunterdon, Somerset and Middlesex Counties. The snow showers started around 4 a.m. EST in far northwest New Jersey and not until around 7 a.m. EST in the Raritan Basin. Snow showers ended between 10 a.m. EST and Noon EST on the 18th. While there were snow showers farther south in the state, they were lighter and air temperatures were above freezing. Untreated roadways in the northwest part of the state were slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 3.3 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Hampton (Sussex County) and Hope (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County) and Oxford (Warren County), 1.5 inches in Butler (Morris County), 1.1 inches in Sparta (Sussex County) and Succasunna (Morris County), 1.0 inch in Hackettstown (Warren County) and 0.5 inches in Bridgewater (Somerset County) and Bethlehem Township (Hunterdon County).

A moist, southwest flow of air preceding a cold front dropped one to three inches of snow across most of New Jersey (except the southeast part of the state) during the daytime hours on the 25th. While less snow accumulated on roadways, untreated and less traveled ones became slippery.  Snow began on the 25th during the middle of the morning in far northwest New Jersey, during the late morning in the Raritan Basin and central New Jersey, around Noon EST in southwest New Jersey and during the early afternoon in southeast New Jersey.  In spite of the different start times, most of the heaviest snow occurred during the first half of the afternoon. The steady snow ended between 5 p.m. and 6 p.m. EST on the 25th statewide.  However, isolated snow showers caused additional light accumulations as the cold front itself moved through that night.| |Representative snowfall included 2.9 inches in Pennsuaken (Camden County), 2.8 inches in Allamuchy (Warren County), 2.7 inches in Montague (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Pennsville (Salem County), 2.4 inches in Ewing (Mercer County), 2.2 inches in Somerdale (Camden County), 2.1 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 2.0 inches in Lebanon (Hunterdon County), Somerset (Somerset County), Randolph (Morris County), National Park (Gloucester County), Metuchen (Middlesex County), Florence (Burlington County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.9 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Moorestown (Burlington County), Freehold (Monmouth County), and Hopewell (Mercer County), 1.7 inches in Jefferson Township (Morris County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 1.5 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 0.8 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Bridgeton (Cumberland County) and 0.4 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport.

A winter storm dropped heavy snow across New Jersey and in particular along the Interstate 95 corridor northeast through Monmouth County from the morning of the 21st into the early morning on the 22nd.  Snowfall totals in this corridor averaged 10 to 16 inches with lower amounts in the northwest and southeast parts of the state. The heavy snow resulted in New Jersey Governor Chris Christie in declaring a state of emergency as state offices were closed early on the 21st. Many schools, courts, government offices, businesses and malls closed early on the 21st and this led to long traffic delays because of the coincidental early releases. Snow removal crews found it difficult to plow on congested roadways. New Jersey State Police reported over 400 accidents and assisted over 800 stranded drivers. One of the worst accidents occurred in Roxbury Township (Morris County) on eastbound Interstate 80 when a charter bus slid down an embankment and struck a tree. Four people were injured. Big delays were reported on the New Jersey Turnpike, the Garden State Parkway, Interstates 78 and 287 and New Jersey State Routes 35 and 66.  The Manasquan Inlet Bridge (New Jersey State Route 35) into Brielle (Monmouth County) was closed.  Many municipal meetings on the evening of the 21st were cancelled. Flights were cancelled out of Mercer County Airport. New Jersey Transit commuter rail service converted their train schedule to enhanced weekend. Twenty to thirty minute system-wide delays were still common.||On the 22nd, many schools throughout the state were closed. Trash and recycling schedules had to be adjusted. New Jersey Transit cross-honored tickets on all forms of transportation.  Many malls and government offices opened late.| |The winter storm was caused by a quick moving clipper system tracked out of the Great Lakes region on the 21st and began transferring its energy to the developing coastal low, resulting in rapid intensification.  This strengthening low pressure moved northeast, allowing multiple bands of heavy snow to traverse across portions of  central New Jersey during the mid-day hours.  By late that day, the coastal low emerged east of the Delmarva Peninsula and quickly moved off towards New England, bringing an end to the snow from west to east across the region overnight on the 21st. ||Along and near the Interstate 95 and 295 corridors, temperatures were cold enough for rapid accumulation, and a narrow band of heavy snow developed in this vicinity, extending eastward into Monmouth County, New Jersey.  Within this general area, snowfall rates reached 2 to 3 inches per hour for a time during the midday hours of the 21st, resulting in total snow accumulations of 10 to 14 inches, with portions of Monmouth County, New Jersey receiving nearly 16 inches.  Another band of heavy snow developed during the evening of the 21st and extended into Hunterdon and Somerset Counties.  Snowfall totals averaged 10 inches, with some higher amounts recorded over the higher terrain.  Elsewhere, mainly less than 4 inches of snow fell across far northwest New Jersey. Totals were under 6 inches in southeast New Jersey as temperatures were marginal for larger snow accumulations, and several hours of mixing with rain and sleet were reported.||Representative snowfall The combination of a relatively moist air mass, light winds preceding a warm front and snow cover produced widespread dense fog in southern New Jersey during the morning and in northern New Jersey during the morning and afternoon of the 21st.  The fog contributed to a fatal traffic three vehicle accident involving a school van in Upper Pittsgrove Township in Salem County.  The three vehicles collided near the intersection of U.S. Route 40 and Burlington Road.  A tractor-trailer was traveling west on U.S. Route 40 and a school van was traveling east on the road and followed by another vehicle.  A fourth vehicle was also traveling east on U.S. Route 40 in front of the school van. The fourth vehicle went to make a left turn onto Burlington Road and the tractor-trailer swerved into the eastbound lane to avoid striking the vehicle and in the process collided with the school van. The school van was then rear-ended by the vehicle following it. The 58-year-old woman driver of the school van was killed. Both the tractor-trailer and the other vehicle's drivers suffered non life threatening injuries as did a 17-year-old boy on the school van. The dense fog lifted and dispersed in the southern part of the state as a warm front moved through, but the northern part of the state was foggy until a cold front moved through the state during the afternoon.

For the second morning in a row, wintry weather affected northwestern New Jersey on the 19th. The air mass aloft was warmer and the wintry precipitation was more of a mixture of sleet and freezing rain with some snow occurring over the higher terrain of Sussex County.  Snowfall averaged an inch or less in the higher terrain of Sussex County. Ice accumulations averaged around one tenth of an inch. Untreated roadways and sidewalks became slippery and hazardous.||Precipitation started briefly as sleet between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the morning of the 19th. The precipitation then changed to freezing rain. In Sussex County, as the precipitation intensity increased, the freezing rain changed to snow over the higher terrain. The snow changed back to sleet and freezing rain before ending early in the afternoon. Elsewhere, as warmer air reached the surface, the freezing rain changed over to plain rain by Noon EST. The rain also ended during the early afternoon.| |Representative snowfall included 1.0 inch in Wantage (Sussex County) and 0.7 inches in Montague (Sussex County). Ice accumulations included about one tenth of an inch in Sussex (Sussex County).  The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a southwest flow of milder and moister air accompanying a warm front that was located in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 19th. The warm front reached central New Jersey during the early afternoon of the 19th. By doing so, it brought above freezing temperatures into the area and also its proximity ended the necessary lift needed to generate precipitation.

A moist, southwest flow of air preceding a cold front dropped one to three inches of snow across most of New Jersey (except the southeast part of the state) during the daytime hours on the 25th. While less snow accumulated on roadways, untreated and less traveled ones became slippery.  Snow began on the 25th during the middle of the morning in far northwest New Jersey, during the late morning in the Raritan Basin and central New Jersey, around Noon EST in southwest New Jersey and during the early afternoon in southeast New Jersey.  In spite of the different start times, most of the heaviest snow occurred during the first half of the afternoon. The steady snow ended between 5 p.m. and 6 p.m. EST on the 25th statewide.  However, isolated snow showers caused additional light accumulations as the cold front itself moved through that night.| |Representative snowfall included 2.9 inches in Pennsuaken (Camden County), 2.8 inches in Allamuchy (Warren County), 2.7 inches in Montague (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Pennsville (Salem County), 2.4 inches in Ewing (Mercer County), 2.2 inches in Somerdale (Camden County), 2.1 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 2.0 inches in Lebanon (Hunterdon County), Somerset (Somerset County), Randolph (Morris County), National Park (Gloucester County), Metuchen (Middlesex County), Florence (Burlington County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.9 inches in Highland Lakes (Sussex County), 1.8 inches in Moorestown (Burlington County), Freehold (Monmouth County), and Hopewell (Mercer County), 1.7 inches in Jefferson Township (Morris County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 1.5 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 0.8 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Bridgeton (Cumberland County) and 0.4 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport.

A cold front coupled with an upper air disturbance triggered bands of accumulating snow showers across the Raritan Basin and Northwest New Jersey on the morning of the 18th. Snowfall averaged one to three inches in Sussex and Warren Counties and around one inch. In Morris, Hunterdon, Somerset and Middlesex Counties. The snow showers started around 4 a.m. EST in far northwest New Jersey and not until around 7 a.m. EST in the Raritan Basin. Snow showers ended between 10 a.m. EST and Noon EST on the 18th. While there were snow showers farther south in the state, they were lighter and air temperatures were above freezing. Untreated roadways in the northwest part of the state were slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 3.3 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Hampton (Sussex County) and Hope (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County) and Oxford (Warren County), 1.5 inches in Butler (Morris County), 1.1 inches in Sparta (Sussex County) and Succasunna (Morris County), 1.0 inch in Hackettstown (Warren County) and 0.5 inches in Bridgewater (Somerset County) and Bethlehem Township (Hunterdon County).

A winter storm dropped heavy snow across New Jersey and in particular along the Interstate 95 corridor northeast through Monmouth County from the morning of the 21st into the early morning on the 22nd.  Snowfall totals in this corridor averaged 10 to 16 inches with lower amounts in the northwest and southeast parts of the state. The heavy snow resulted in New Jersey Governor Chris Christie in declaring a state of emergency as state offices were closed early on the 21st. Many schools, courts, government offices, businesses and malls closed early on the 21st and this led to long traffic delays because of the coincidental early releases. Snow removal crews found it difficult to plow on congested roadways. New Jersey State Police reported over 400 accidents and assisted over 800 stranded drivers. One of the worst accidents occurred in Roxbury Township (Morris County) on eastbound Interstate 80 when a charter bus slid down an embankment and struck a tree. Four people were injured. Big delays were reported on the New Jersey Turnpike, the Garden State Parkway, Interstates 78 and 287 and New Jersey State Routes 35 and 66.  The Manasquan Inlet Bridge (New Jersey State Route 35) into Brielle (Monmouth County) was closed.  Many municipal meetings on the evening of the 21st were cancelled. Flights were cancelled out of Mercer County Airport. New Jersey Transit commuter rail service converted their train schedule to enhanced weekend. Twenty to thirty minute system-wide delays were still common.||On the 22nd, many schools throughout the state were closed. Trash and recycling schedules had to be adjusted. New Jersey Transit cross-honored tickets on all forms of transportation.  Many malls and government offices opened late.| |The winter storm was caused by a quick moving clipper system tracked out of the Great Lakes region on the 21st and began transferring its energy to the developing coastal low, resulting in rapid intensification.  This strengthening low pressure moved northeast, allowing multiple bands of heavy snow to traverse across portions of  central New Jersey during the mid-day hours.  By late that day, the coastal low emerged east of the Delmarva Peninsula and quickly moved off towards New England, bringing an end to the snow from west to east across the region overnight on the 21st. ||Along and near the Interstate 95 and 295 corridors, temperatures were cold enough for rapid accumulation, and a narrow band of heavy snow developed in this vicinity, extending eastward into Monmouth County, New Jersey.  Within this general area, snowfall rates reached 2 to 3 inches per hour for a time during the midday hours of the 21st, resulting in total snow accumulations of 10 to 14 inches, with portions of Monmouth County, New Jersey receiving nearly 16 inches.  Another band of heavy snow developed during the evening of the 21st and extended into Hunterdon and Somerset Counties.  Snowfall totals averaged 10 inches, with some higher amounts recorded over the higher terrain.  Elsewhere, mainly less than 4 inches of snow fell across far northwest New Jersey. Totals were under 6 inches in southeast New Jersey as temperatures were marginal for larger snow accumulations, and several hours of mixing with rain and sleet were reported.||Representative snowfall Heavy snow fell across southeast New Jersey during the evening and overnight on the 28th. Lighter accumulating snow fell across the rest of New Jersey southeast of the New Jersey Turnpike while even lighter snow (generally less than an inch) fell in locations northwest of the New Jersey Turnpike. Snow started during the late evening on the 28th and ended around sunrise on the 29th. Snowfall averaged 4 to 7 inches in Cape May, Cumberland,  Atlantic and Ocean Counties where the heavy snow fell. Progressively lighter snowfall amounts occurred farther to the north and west.  Snow to liquid water equivalent ratios were high with this event, creating an incredibly dry and fluffy snow. Ratios across southern New Jersey ranged from around 20:1 to 30:1, with closer to 10:1 to 20:1 ratios farther inland and northward. One person used his leaf blower to clear three inches of snow in Manasquan (Monmouth County).|  |With cold temperatures in place, snow accumulated quickly leading to slippery and snow covered roadways.  There were speed reductions along the Garden State Parkway and New Jersey Turnpike. Because the snow fell overnight, the total number of accidents was relatively low.  The overnight snow caused schools in the southern part of the state to have delayed openings on the morning of the 29th.  Following this system, an arctic air mass, coupled with the snow cover, produced unseasonably cold temperatures. | |Representative snowfall totals included 7.5 inches in Surf City (Ocean County), 7.3 inches at the Atlantic City Airport and Pleasantville (Atlantic County), 7.0 inches in Estell Manor and Galloway Township (Atlantic County), 6.8 inches in Marmora (Cape May County), 6.5 inches in Ship Bottom and Little Egg Harbor Township (Ocean County) and Dennis Township (Cape May County), 5.0 inches in Millville (Cumberland County) and Pittsgrove (Salem County), 4.1 inches in Bridgeton (Cumberland County), 4.0 inches in Malaga (Gloucester County), 3.3 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 2.5 inches in Tabernacle (Burlington County), 2.3 inches in Neptune and Howell in Monmouth County, 1.8 inches in Woodstown (Salem County), 1.7 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County) and Medford Lakes (Burlington County) and 1.4 inches in Cherry Hill (Camden County).||The snow was caused  by a low pressure that formed along a front in the northern Gulf of Mexico on the 28th. By the night of the 28th, the low pressure system had moved off the Carolina coast and precipitation had reached the state. However, dry air across the region from a high pressure system over New England and the Ohio Valley limited the heavy snow to the southeast part of the state. This low pressure system quickly moved off the Carolina coastline the morning of the 29th, with bitterly cold air moving in behind it.

The combination of a relatively moist air mass, light winds preceding a warm front and snow cover produced widespread dense fog in southern New Jersey during the morning and in northern New Jersey during the morning and afternoon of the 21st.  The fog contributed to a fatal traffic three vehicle accident involving a school van in Upper Pittsgrove Township in Salem County.  The three vehicles collided near the intersection of U.S. Route 40 and Burlington Road.  A tractor-trailer was traveling west on U.S. Route 40 and a school van was traveling east on the road and followed by another vehicle.  A fourth vehicle was also traveling east on U.S. Route 40 in front of the school van. The fourth vehicle went to make a left turn onto Burlington Road and the tractor-trailer swerved into the eastbound lane to avoid striking the vehicle and in the process collided with the school van. The school van was then rear-ended by the vehicle following it. The 58-year-old woman driver of the school van was killed. Both the tractor-trailer and the other vehicle's drivers suffered non life threatening injuries as did a 17-year-old boy on the school van. The dense fog lifted and dispersed in the southern part of the state as a warm front moved through, but the northern part of the state was foggy until a cold front moved through the state during the afternoon.

The fog contributed to a fatal traffic three vehicle accident involving a school van in Upper Pittsgrove Township in Salem County.  The three vehicles collided near the intersection of U.S. Route 40 and Burlington Road.  A tractor-trailer was traveling west on U.S. Route 40 and a school van was traveling east on the road and followed by another vehicle.  A fourth vehicle was also traveling east on U.S. Route 40 in front of the school van. The fourth vehicle went to make a left turn onto Burlington Road and the tractor-trailer swerved into the eastbound lane to avoid striking the vehicle and in the process collided with the school van. The school van was then rear-ended by the vehicle following it. The 58-year-old woman driver of the school van was killed. Both the tractor-trailer and the other vehicle's drivers suffered non life threatening injuries as did a 17-year-old boy on the school van. The dense fog lifted and dispersed in the southern part of the state as a warm front moved through, but the northern part of the state was foggy until a cold front moved through the state during the afternoon.

A developing cold front triggered snow showers in New Jersey during the morning into the early afternoon of the 26th. The snow showers and snowfall were greatest in the Passaic and Raritan Basins and the Philadelphia eastern and northeastern suburbs averaging 1 to 2 inches. Elsewhere in New Jersey, snowfall averaged less than 1 inch. The snow showers started between 6 a.m. and 9 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest in the second half of the morning and finished moving through between Noon and 1 p.m. EST that afternoon. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Randolph Township (Morris County), 1.8 inches in Frenchtown (Hunterdon County), 1.6 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Medford (Burlington County), Montgomery (Somerset County), South Plainfield (Middlesex County), Mendham (Morris County)  and Somerdale (Camden County), 1.3 inches in North Brunswick (Middlesex County), 1.1 inches in National Park (Gloucester County), 1.0 inch in Hopewell Township (Mercer County) and 0.3 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through the state from the northwest to southeast between 9 a.m. EST and 1 p.m. EST on the 26th and nearly coincided with the end of the snow showers.

Heavy snow fell across southeast New Jersey during the evening and overnight on the 28th. Lighter accumulating snow fell across the rest of New Jersey southeast of the New Jersey Turnpike while even lighter snow (generally less than an inch) fell in locations northwest of the New Jersey Turnpike. Snow started during the late evening on the 28th and ended around sunrise on the 29th. Snowfall averaged 4 to 7 inches in Cape May, Cumberland,  Atlantic and Ocean Counties where the heavy snow fell. Progressively lighter snowfall amounts occurred farther to the north and west.  Snow to liquid water equivalent ratios were high with this event, creating an incredibly dry and fluffy snow. Ratios across southern New Jersey ranged from around 20:1 to 30:1, with closer to 10:1 to 20:1 ratios farther inland and northward. One person used his leaf blower to clear three inches of snow in Manasquan (Monmouth County).|  |With cold temperatures in place, snow accumulated quickly leading to slippery and snow covered roadways.  There were speed reductions along the Garden State Parkway and New Jersey Turnpike. Because the snow fell overnight, the total number of accidents was relatively low.  The overnight snow caused schools in the southern part of the state to have delayed openings on the morning of the 29th.  Following this system, an arctic air mass, coupled with the snow cover, produced unseasonably cold temperatures. | |Representative snowfall totals included 7.5 inches in Surf City (Ocean County), 7.3 inches at the Atlantic City Airport and Pleasantville (Atlantic County), 7.0 inches in Estell Manor and Galloway Township (Atlantic County), 6.8 inches in Marmora (Cape May County), 6.5 inches in Ship Bottom and Little Egg Harbor Township (Ocean County) and Dennis Township (Cape May County), 5.0 inches in Millville (Cumberland County) and Pittsgrove (Salem County), 4.1 inches in Bridgeton (Cumberland County), 4.0 inches in Malaga (Gloucester County), 3.3 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 2.5 inches in Tabernacle (Burlington County), 2.3 inches in Neptune and Howell in Monmouth County, 1.8 inches in Woodstown (Salem County), 1.7 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County) and Medford Lakes (Burlington County) and 1.4 inches in Cherry Hill (Camden County).||The snow was caused  by a low pressure that formed along a front in the northern Gulf of Mexico on the 28th. By the night of the 28th, the low pressure system had moved off the Carolina coast and precipitation had reached the state. However, dry air across the region from a high pressure system over New England and the Ohio Valley limited the heavy snow to the southeast part of the state. This low pressure system quickly moved off the Carolina coastline the morning of the 29th, with bitterly cold air moving in behind it.

For the second morning in a row, wintry weather affected northwestern New Jersey on the 19th. The air mass aloft was warmer and the wintry precipitation was more of a mixture of sleet and freezing rain with some snow occurring over the higher terrain of Sussex County.  Snowfall averaged an inch or less in the higher terrain of Sussex County. Ice accumulations averaged around one tenth of an inch. Untreated roadways and sidewalks became slippery and hazardous.||Precipitation started briefly as sleet between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the morning of the 19th. The precipitation then changed to freezing rain. In Sussex County, as the precipitation intensity increased, the freezing rain changed to snow over the higher terrain. The rain also ended during the early afternoon.| |Representative snowfall included 1.0 inch in Wantage (Sussex County) and 0.7 inches in Montague (Sussex County). Ice accumulations included about one tenth of an inch in Sussex (Sussex County).  The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a southwest flow of milder and moister air accompanying a warm front that was located in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 19th. The warm front reached central New Jersey during the early afternoon of the 19th. By doing so, it brought above freezing temperatures into the area and also its proximity ended the necessary lift needed to generate precipitation.

The combination of a relatively moist air mass, light winds preceding a warm front and snow cover produced widespread dense fog in southern New Jersey during the morning and in northern New Jersey during the morning and afternoon of the 21st.  The fog contributed to a fatal traffic three vehicle accident involving a school van in Upper Pittsgrove Township in Salem County.  The three vehicles collided near the intersection of U.S. Route 40 and Burlington Road.  A tractor-trailer was traveling west on U.S. Route 40 and a school van was traveling east on the road and followed by another vehicle.  A fourth vehicle was also traveling east on U.S. Route 40 in front of the school van. The fourth vehicle went to make a left turn onto Burlington Road and the tractor-trailer swerved into the eastbound lane to avoid striking the vehicle and in the process collided with the school van. The school van was then rear-ended by the vehicle following it. The 58-year-old woman driver of the school van was killed. Both the tractor-trailer and the other vehicle's drivers suffered non life threatening injuries as did a 17-year-old boy on the school van. The dense fog lifted and dispersed in the southern part of the state as a warm front moved through, but the northern part of the state was foggy until a cold front moved through the state during the afternoon.
